title: Ajwain Water
content: Boil 1 teaspoon of ajwain (carom seeds) in 1 cup of water for 5 minutes. Strain and drink warm. Helps relieve menstrual cramps within 20-30 minutes. Safe to drink twice a day during periods.

title: Ginger Jaggery Tea
content: Boil a 1-inch piece of fresh ginger in 2 cups of water for 10 minutes. Add 1 teaspoon of jaggery and stir. Drink warm. Relieves bloating, nausea and cramps. Drink up to 3 times a day.

title: Heat Therapy
content: Apply a warm cloth or heating pad to the lower abdomen or lower back for 15-20 minutes. Relieves muscle tension and cramps. Repeat every 2-3 hours. Always use a cloth barrier — do not apply heat directly to bare skin.

title: Fetal Sleep Position
content: Lie on your side and draw your knees up toward your chest. This relaxes the muscles around your pelvis and reduces cramp intensity. Best sleep position during periods.

title: Yoga for Period Pain
content: Child's pose: Kneel, sit back on heels, stretch arms forward on floor, hold 30 seconds. Cat-cow pose: On hands and knees, arch and round your back slowly 10 times. Both ease lower back pain and pelvic tension.

title: Warm Water Hydration
content: Drink warm or room-temperature water throughout your period. Warm water improves circulation and relaxes muscles, reducing cramps. Aim for 8-10 glasses per day. Avoid cold drinks during heavy flow days.

title: Foods to Avoid During Periods
content: Avoid: excess salt (causes bloating), caffeine like tea and coffee (worsens cramps), cold foods and drinks (tightens muscles), processed and junk food (increases inflammation), alcohol (worsens mood and dehydration).

title: Foods to Eat During Periods
content: Eat: bananas (potassium, reduces cramps), dark chocolate 70%+ (magnesium, improves mood), spinach and methi (iron, fights fatigue), almonds and flaxseed (omega-3, reduces inflammation), turmeric milk at night for pain relief.

title: Reducing Period Bloating
content: Drink warm saunf (fennel seed) water — boil 1 tsp saunf in water and drink. Avoid salt and salty snacks. Light walking for 10-15 minutes helps. Avoid carbonated drinks completely.

title: Dealing with Period Fatigue
content: Period fatigue is caused by iron loss during bleeding. Eat iron-rich foods: spinach, rajma, chana, dates (khajur), jaggery. Sleep 7-9 hours. Light activity is better than complete rest. Stay hydrated with warm fluids.

title: Managing Mood Swings and PMS
content: Mood swings are caused by hormonal changes — completely normal. Eat magnesium-rich foods: dark chocolate, almonds, bananas. Avoid caffeine and alcohol. Light exercise releases endorphins. Practice deep breathing: inhale 4 counts, hold 4, exhale 6.

title: Sanitary Pad Hygiene
content: Change pads every 4-6 hours regardless of flow. Leaving pads too long causes infections and rashes. Use overnight pads at night and change first thing in the morning. Always wash hands before and after changing.

title: Menstrual Cup Use
content: Empty menstrual cups every 8-12 hours. Wash with mild soap and warm water. Sterilize by boiling in water for 5 minutes before first use each cycle. Cups are reusable for up to 10 years and eco-friendly.

title: Safe Disposal of Sanitary Products
content: Wrap used pads in the wrapper of the new pad or newspaper before disposing. Never flush pads down the toilet — they block drains. Dispose in a closed dustbin. Never flush tampons or cups either.

title: Why Periods Become Irregular
content: Common causes: high stress, sudden weight gain or loss, PCOS, thyroid issues, over-exercising, poor sleep, nutritional deficiencies. Occasional irregularity of 1-2 weeks is normal. If consistently irregular for 3+ months, consult a gynaecologist.

title: Home Remedies for Irregular Periods
content: Eat sesame seeds (til) with jaggery daily 2 weeks before expected period. Drink cinnamon (dalchini) tea — boil 1 stick in water — once daily. Maintain consistent sleep. Manage stress through yoga. These support hormonal balance but are not medical treatments.

title: Managing Heavy Period Flow
content: Heavy flow can cause fatigue and anaemia. Increase iron: spinach, rajma, dates. Stay hydrated. Avoid aspirin — it worsens bleeding. Cold compress on lower abdomen can temporarily reduce heavy flow. If soaking more than one pad per hour for 2+ hours, seek medical help immediately.

title: Better Sleep During Periods
content: Use a heating pad on low setting while sleeping. Wear comfortable loose clothing. Sleep in fetal position. Keep room slightly cool. Drink warm turmeric milk before bed. Place a towel under you if worried about leaking overnight.

title: When to Take Pain Medicine
content: Ibuprofen (Combiflam or Brufen) is safe and effective for period pain. Take with food, not on empty stomach. Do not exceed recommended dose. Avoid aspirin as it increases bleeding. If you need medicine every cycle or it stops working, consult a doctor.

title: When to See a Doctor Immediately
content: See a doctor immediately if: fever above 101°F with heavy bleeding, cramps so severe you cannot stand, bleeding soaking more than one pad per hour for 2+ hours, period lasting more than 7 days, sudden sharp abdominal pain, foul-smelling discharge, or fainting.

title: Period Emergency Kit
content: Always carry: 2-3 sanitary pads or tampons, ibuprofen tablet, hand sanitizer, dark-colored spare underwear, disposal bags for used pads, small heating patch. Keep in your bag or school/college locker so you are never unprepared.

title: Common Period Myths Busted
content: Myth: Cannot exercise during periods. Fact: Light exercise reduces cramps. Myth: Period blood is dirty. Fact: It is normal uterine lining. Myth: Should not wash hair during periods. Fact: No medical basis. Myth: Irregular periods always mean PCOS. Fact: Many causes exist, only a doctor can diagnose PCOS.

title: Myths About Period and Food
content: Myth: Do not eat sour foods during periods. Fact: No evidence sour foods worsen periods. Myth: Pickles stop periods. Fact: Pickles are high in salt causing bloating but don't affect uterus. Myth: Cold food stops periods. Fact: Cold foods may cause temporary muscle tightening but cannot stop your period.

title: You Are Not Alone
content: Periods can be physically and emotionally difficult. What you feel — the pain, mood changes, exhaustion — is real and valid. There is no shame in asking for help or resting. Sakhi is here for you. You are part of a community of women who support each other.

title: What is the Sakhi Network
content: Sakhi is a peer-to-peer period emergency network. If you run out of sanitary products, request help from verified Sakhi volunteers near you. All helpers are verified women. Your location is only shared when you send a request and is removed after the emergency is resolved.

title: How to Request Help on Sakhi
content: To request period supplies: open the app, tap SOS button, select what you need (pad, tampon, or cup), confirm your location, and a verified volunteer near you will be notified. After receiving help, pay it forward by becoming a Sakhi helper yourself.
